key_insights.insight: Stress is not inherently negative; it becomes detrimental when it leads to burnout or anticipatory anxiety.
key_insights.quote: Stress is very good for you until it kills you.

key_insights.insight: The pursuit of endless growth and progress can be a harmful 'lie' leading to overwork and a neglect of well-being and genuine connection.
key_insights.quote: The whole endless cycle of growth and progress is a big lie... More is better. Faster is better.

key_insights.insight: Humans often make choices that increase stress, even when aware of the consequences, due to being trapped in a cycle and overestimating their capacity.
key_insights.quote: We make those choices, not because we're not intelligent enough to recognize the impact. We make those choices because we are so caught up in that cycle.

key_insights.insight: Much of work is performative, with only a small fraction contributing to actual output.
key_insights.quote: Eighty percent of the stuff you do at work is just to prove you're alive, okay twenty percent of what you do at work actually achieves the numbers.

key_insights.insight: True productivity comes from carving out stress-free time for creativity, connection, and meaningful work, rather than overloading the calendar.
key_insights.quote: The true productivity is that one hour in the morning where you're not stressed, where your calendar is not loaded... Or you write down a concept that fits in your next book.

key_insights.insight: Burnout is not caused by a single event, but by the accumulation of numerous small stressors (nuisances) over time.
key_insights.quote: The burnout equation is the number of stressors multiplied by the intensity of each multiplied by the time of application of each multiplied by the frequency of application.

key_insights.insight: Stress is a perceived ratio, not an absolute: stress = challenges / resources. Increasing resources makes challenges less stressful.
key_insights.quote: Our stress as humans... is the intensity of the challenges that you face, divided by the skills and resources and the abilities that you have to deal with that stress.

key_insights.insight: The biggest component of dealing with stress is limiting stressors, as 80% of them are unnecessary.
key_insights.quote: 80% of that person's life is not needed.

key_insights.insight: Limiting stressors involves deliberately taking inventory and eliminating nuisances that creep into daily life, including draining friendships.
key_insights.quote: If we're able to look at those stressors and say, hold on, I'm going to take an inventory of all of the things that stressed me last week...And I'm going to tell myself, oh, by the way, I don't need this. I don't need this.

key_insights.insight: Fear manifests in derivatives: worry, anxiety and panic, each triggered by different primary concerns (challenge, uncertainty, ability, and time, respectively).
key_insights.quote: Fear is a question of a challenge... Worry is a question of uncertainty.Anxiety is a question of your ability.And panic is a question of, do I have enough time?

actionable_advice.advice: If a thought is causing mental stress and waking you up at night, write it down and promise yourself you will think about it in the morning.
actionable_advice.how_to: Write the thought down, promise yourself that you're going to think about it in the morning before you go to bed, and keep that promise.

actionable_advice.advice: Intentionally create a support network and reach out to people to question your thoughts and express and share them rather than block them.
actionable_advice.how_to: Use the technique 'meet Becky', the idea that you allow your brain to express things and share them rather than block them.

actionable_advice.advice: Actively do a body scan to spot your own physical stress.
actionable_advice.how_to: Close your eyes, take a deep breath and scan your body from the top of your head all the way to your toes and see where it hurts.

actionable_advice.advice: Rank the things that are important in your life and only focus on those.
actionable_advice.how_to: Rank your priorities in life and cut the less important items and make space to chill and find expansion in our life

actionable_advice.advice: Determine the root cause of the worry and take necessary action.
actionable_advice.how_to: Turn the worry into certainty, whether that's the best certainty or the worst certainty. But then react to the certainty

actionable_advice.advice: Reduce the number of things that you don't use or love and get rid of them.
actionable_advice.how_to: Every Saturday throw/giveaway 10 things away from your home.

memorable_stories: The story of the billionaire fisherman who already lived the peaceful life the billionaire advised him to strive for.
The story of his ex-wife, Ibel, flipping through photos of their son Ali, pointing out that each phase of his life ended with his death, underscoring the finite nature of seasons.
His time in LA detention was some of the best 37 hours he had. No technology, no phone, nothing.
contrarian_claims: That one should aim to marry someone before knowing them that long.
Economic conflict is not the reason for wars, but rather it's the reverse: wars are started as a reason for economic means.
AI will make the world a more difficult place, and the objective should be to learn how to deal with it.
The idea that productivity is a good thing and something that we need more of is a big lie.
That, he, Mo Gawdat, wanted to die a billionaire. But with happiness as the currency.
episode_title: The Diary Of A CEO with Steven Bartlett - Mo Gawdat: 80% Of Illness Is Linked To One Thing! An Alarming Warning For The Burnout Generation! If You Feel Like This, Quit Your Job Today!
